title: Why the trigger of being in a position of authority feels fraudulent
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You get the promotion. And your body says no. 

 You worked for this. Years of showing up, keeping your head down, proving yourself. Then the offer comes....
htmlContent:
You get the promotion. And your body says no.

You worked for this. Years of showing up, keeping your head down, proving yourself. Then the offer comes. The title. The team. The authority. And something inside you goes ice cold.

I remember the first time someone called me "boss." I flinched. Literally flinched. My shoulders went up toward my ears, my stomach dropped, and I had this overwhelming urge to say "Oh no, I'm not actually in charge here, I just look like I am." I wanted to hand the title back. I wanted to explain that it was a mistake. That they had the wrong person.

Here's the thing. That feeling isn't a character flaw. It isn't imposter syndrome in the way people usually talk about it, where you just need a little confidence boost and a good affirmation. What you're feeling is something older, something wired into your nervous system from a time when being seen, being visible, being in a position of authority meant you were in danger.

Stay with me here. Because this gets specific. And it might change how you see that knot in your chest every time you have to make a decision someone else depends on.

The nervous system doesn't know the difference between a boardroom and a battlefield

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system scans for safety and danger. He calls this process neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness. Your body is constantly asking: "Am I safe right now? Can I relax? Or do I need to prepare for threat?"

Here's what's wild. Your nervous system learned its threat-detection patterns years ago, probably in childhood. If you grew up in an environment where authority figures were unpredictable, harsh, or dangerous, then being in a position of authority yourself becomes a trigger. Not because you're weak. Because your body remembers what happened to people who had power. And it's trying to protect you from becoming one of them.

Think about it. If your parent or caregiver was the person who could hurt you, then authority equals danger. Full stop. Your nervous system doesn't care that you're now an adult with a corner office and a LinkedIn profile. It cares about survival. And survival says: don't be the one in charge. That's where the abuse lives. That's where people get punished. That's where you become the thing that hurt you.

So you show up to a meeting where you're supposed to delegate tasks, and your throat closes up. You have to give feedback to a direct report, and your heart starts hammering. You sit in the chair at the head of the table, and you feel like a child wearing their parent's shoes. Too big. Wrong. A fraud waiting to be exposed.

That's not imposter syndrome. That's a trauma response.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, talks about how trauma lives in the body as sensation, not as memory you can think your way out of. You can't logic your way into feeling legitimate as a leader. You can't affirm your way out of a nervous system that believes authority will get you killed or abandoned.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). It helps with the physical jitteriness that comes with activation. But supplements alone won't rewire the core belief. You have to go deeper.

The fraud is a protector, not a flaw

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), would say that the part of you that feels fraudulent isn't your enemy. It's a protector. It's a part that learned, probably very early, that staying small kept you safe. That if you didn't assert yourself, didn't take up space, didn't claim power, then maybe you wouldn't get hurt.

That part is doing its job. It's just doing a job that no longer applies.

I've sat with this part in myself many times. It shows up as a tightness in my chest, a voice that says "Who do you think you are?" It has a lot of energy behind it. It's scared. It's scared that if I actually believe I deserve to be in charge, someone will come along and prove me wrong. Publicly. Cruelly.

And here's the part that hurts. That voice isn't wrong about the past. It was right then. Staying small kept you safe. But it's wrong about now. And the only way to help that part relax is to show it, over and over, that authority doesn't have to mean cruelty. That you can be in charge without becoming the person who hurt you.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. Moving between the felt sense of threat and the felt sense of safety, slowly, rhythmically, until your nervous system learns that safety exists even when you're visible. You can practice this in small ways. Hold eye contact with someone for an extra second. Speak your opinion in a meeting without apologizing first. Let someone disagree with you and notice that you don't die.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It can take the edge off the hypervigilance without making you drowsy. But again, the real work is in the body, in the practice of staying present when your system wants to flee.

Why authority triggers shame more than fear

Here's something I didn't expect. When I finally started to believe I deserved to be in charge, the fear didn't go away. It transformed into shame. A deep, quiet shame that whispered: "You're being arrogant. You're acting like you're better than everyone. You're going to become exactly what you hate."

That shame is a trap. It's the legacy of being raised by people who punished you for taking up space. Who told you that wanting things was selfish. That having power meant you were bad. That the only safe way to exist was to be small, helpful, and invisible.

Janina Fisher, a trauma expert who works with structural dissociation, calls this the "inner critic" or the "exile" part that carries the shame of being seen. She says that when we step into authority, we activate the part of us that was shamed for existing. And that part floods us with feelings of fraudulence not because we are fraudulent, but because we were treated as though we were.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did you feel a wave of "Yeah but that's not me, I actually am not good enough"? That's the shame talking. It's loud. It's convincing. And it's not the truth.

You can work with this shame directly. One way is to name it out loud. Say to yourself: "I feel like a fraud because I was taught that taking power is dangerous. That is a belief from my past, not a fact about my present." Say it again. Let it land.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). Sometimes the shame gets louder when your nervous system is overwhelmed by sensory input. Reducing that input can give you a little more room to breathe and think.

You don't have to be a good leader. You just have to be a real one.

I know you want to be the kind of leader you never had. The kind who is fair, kind, grounded, and calm. And that's a beautiful intention. But here's the hard truth: you can't lead from a place of pretending you're not triggered. You have to lead from the place of being triggered and still showing up.

That means you might shake during a difficult conversation. You might forget what you were going to say. You might need to excuse yourself to the bathroom and put your hands on the sink and breathe for a minute. That's not failure. That's integration. That's your nervous system learning, in real time, that you can survive being visible.

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "mindsight," talks about integration as the key to mental health. Integration means you don't have to get rid of the parts that feel scared or fraudulent. You just have to let them coexist with the parts that know you're capable. You hold both. You don't deny either.

So the next time you sit in that chair, or step into that room, or make that decision, and the fraud feeling rises up, don't fight it. Don't try to convince yourself you're confident. Instead, say this to yourself: "I see you, scared part. You're trying to protect me. I hear you. And I'm going to do this anyway."

That's the work. It's not about feeling ready. It's about feeling scared and doing it anyway. It's about letting your nervous system learn, one small act of authority at a time, that you are not in danger. That you are not your abuser. That you can hold power and still be safe.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you a framework for working with the shame and fear that come up around authority. It's not a fix. But it's a map.

And honestly? That's enough. A map. A direction. A way to keep walking even when every cell in your body is telling you to run.
